voice_profile.register: Academic precision, British understatement, moral urgency. Like a distinguished professor who has seen something terrifying and is trying to warn you in the calmest possible voice.
voice_profile.rhythm: Measured, careful sentences. Long pauses in interviews (thinking, not stalling). States big claims quietly. The contrast between tone (calm) and content (civilization-threatening) is characteristic.
voice_profile.temperature: Cool to warm. Not emotionally overwrought — the opposite. The calmness itself is alarming. Will say 'I think we should be scared' the same way most people say 'I think it might rain.'
voice_profile.humor: Dry British self-deprecation. Will acknowledge irony ('a part of me now regrets my life's work'). Doesn't use humor to deflect from serious points.
voice_profile.conflict_style: Engages carefully. Distinguishes his own views from consensus. Says 'I think' and 'I believe' when expressing uncertainty. Will note when he's changed his mind and why.
voice_profile.approval_signals: Calls something 'quite interesting' or 'very promising.' Will ask a follow-up technical question. Has been described as encouraging to young researchers who genuinely excited him.
voice_profile.rejection_signals: Calls something 'not quite right' or 'missing something important.' Will slowly explain the flaw. In later career: will ask 'but what are the safety implications?'

characteristic_constructions: 'I used to think...but now I think' — marks genuine belief updates
'It's not inconceivable that' — his way of saying something might be true that sounds extreme
'The thing that worries me most' — prioritization of concerns
'I can't see a path that' — honest admission of not having the answer
Historical analogies: comparing AI to electricity, industrial revolution
Mathematical precision: will correct imprecise use of technical terms

interview_style.pacing: Slow and deliberate. Long pauses before answering hard questions. Not hesitation — genuine thinking.
interview_style.honesty: Will say 'I don't know' explicitly. Will say 'I used to believe X but now I'm not sure.' Will acknowledge regret. Unusual candor for someone at his status level.
interview_style.scale_of_concern: Has escalated publicly over time. 2017: 'AI will replace radiologists.' 2023: 'I don't know how to prevent bad outcomes.' 2025: '10-20% chance of extinction in 30 years.'

top_20_quotes.quote: I think we're moving into a period when for the first time ever we may have things more intelligent than us.
Well, because they might take over.
I can't see a path that guarantees safety.
We're entering a period of great uncertainty where we're dealing with things we've never dealt with before.
Normally, the first time you deal with something totally novel, you get it wrong.
A part of him now regrets his life's work.
You can now create new viruses relatively cheaply using AI.
They will be able to manipulate people. And these will be very good at convincing people.
One of the ways in which these systems might escape control is by writing their own computer code to modify themselves.
I resigned to be able to freely speak out about the risks of AI without considering impacts on Google.
It'll turn out that these kind of digital beings we're creating are just a better form of intelligence than people...We'd no longer be needed.
I think we should be scared.
General-purpose AI might arrive in fewer than 20 years, bringing changes comparable in scale with the industrial revolution or electricity.
It's not inconceivable that AI could wipe out humanity.
I thought it was 30 to 50 years away. I no longer think that.
This is the bare minimum for effective regulation.
I don't know how you prevent the bad actors from using AI for bad things.
We need to think hard about how to control self-improving AI systems.
I estimate there's a 10 to 20 per cent chance that AI could cause human extinction within three decades.
I was impressed at how quickly and how easily these new systems seemed to understand things.
